all_recent.body:
*** Huntress is performing Assisted Remediations. An update will be provided upon completion. Please review any Manual Remediations below. ***

Host: JDH-DC - https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/agents/11359368
Organization: JDH - JDH Pacific
Tags: None
Security Products: Trend Micro (ntrtscan)

Incident Report: https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/infection_reports/1946260
Severity: Low

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
At 2026-02-11 04:51:47 UTC, Huntress detected malicious persistence on host "JDH-DC" under the user account "JDH\johne". A registry Run key entry named "Windows Logon Process" was configured to execute "winlgn.exe" from a non-standard directory, masquerading as a legitimate Windows component. This persistence mechanism ensures the malicious executable will launch automatically at user logon.

The investigation revealed that the executable "winlgn.exe" was located in "C:\Users\johne\AppData\Roaming\Update\", a directory commonly abused by malware to evade detection. The file name "winlgn.exe" is designed to mimic the legitimate Windows Logon process, a common obfuscation technique. The persistence was established via a User Run key in the registry at "HKU\S-1-5-21-2000478354-1214440339-682003330-1678\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run". While the binary is no longer present on the system, the registry entry remains active.

The primary risk is that this persistence mechanism was configured to maintain unauthorised access or execute malicious payloads at each user logon. Huntress recommends manually removing the registry Run key entry named "Windows Logon Process" from the affected user's registry hive. Additionally, reset the password for the user account "JDH\johne" and conduct a thorough review of the host for any additional indicators of compromise. Failure to remediate may allow reinfection or continued unauthorised access.

User Account
User Account: "JDH\johne" (SID: S-1-5-21-2000478354-1214440339-682003330-1678)

File System
File Path: "C:\Users\johne\AppData\Roaming\Update\winlgn.exe"
SHA256: Not available

Persistence
Name: "Windows Logon Process"
Type: "User Run Key"
Service Path: "C:\Users\johne\AppData\Roaming\Update\winlgn.exe"
Privilege Level: "User"
Registry Location: "HKU\S-1-5-21-2000478354-1214440339-682003330-1678\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run"

Host
Hostname: "JDH-DC"
Operating System: "Windows Server 2008 R2 Standard"

Threat Descriptions:
--------------------
Malware Artifact: A Malware Artifact is a piece of evidence left behind by malware on a computer system. It could be a file, registry entry, or network connection that indicates the presence of malicious activity.

Remediations:
-------------
Assisted Remediations provided by the Huntress SOC to remediate the incident. These can be executed automatically in the Huntress Platform:
- Delete Registry Value - key: HKU\S-1-5-21-2000478354-1214440339-682003330-1678\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run + value: Windows Logon Process

All provided remediations and recommendations can be found in the Huntress Platform: https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/infection_reports/1946260#remediations-tab

Lead Signal Information:
------------------------
Signal Name: Autorun Inheritance Malicious
Detected At: 2026-02-11 04:49:26 UTC
Registry Key: HKU\S-1-5-21-2000478354-1214440339-682003330-1678\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
Registry Value: Windows Logon Process
Registry Data: C:\Users\johne\AppData\Roaming\Update\winlgn.exe
File Path: 

All investigated signals can be found in the Huntress Platform: https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/infection_reports/1946260#signals-investigated-tab

-------------------------
Thanks again for trusting Huntress and please don't hesitate to reach out to incidents@huntress.io if you have any questions.
all_recent.subject: LOW - Incident on JDH-DC (JDH - JDH Pacific)

all_recent.body:
Host: Donald-WS-Home - https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/agents/11588786
Organization: JDH - JDH Pacific
Tags: None
Security Products: CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor, McAfee

Incident Report: https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/infection_reports/1802192
Severity: Low

Investigative Summary:
----------------------
Huntress detected one or more files on this endpoint that may contain passwords. Having passwords readily available on disk or network shares does not follow security best-practices as threat actors often search for and exploit this data. To reduce organizational risk, we recommend you remove these files and consider the use of a secure password manager.
This message was sent out to ensure you are aware of this potential security risk on this host. We will not be able to verify removal of the password files. We did not collect/examine the file to verify that it contains passwords.

Given the existence of this file, we recommend that your users receive training on the risks of insecure password storage:

- If you already have a Huntress Security Awareness Training (SAT) subscription, you can assign the "Storing Passwords" and "Password Manager" episodes to all employees of the affected organization.
- If you don't have an SAT subscription yet, you can start a free trial for full access to all content. Alternatively, we've made the "Storing Passwords" episode available to you for free at https://mycurricula.com/limited-preview/gplK6A7B8ROq

Commands used to open files that may contain passwords:
User: donaldh
Command: "C:\Program Files\WindowsApps\Microsoft.WindowsNotepad_11.2508.38.0_x64__8wekyb3d8bbwe\Notepad\Notepad.exe" "C:\Users\donaldh\OneDrive - JDH Pacific\Desktop\VPN PASSWORD.txt"

Remediation Instructions:
-------------------------
To mitigate against post-exploitation attacks and lateral movement, consider switching to a secure password management solution. Before deleting any local credential files, ensure they have been backed up in a secure, encrypted location.
There may be other password files located on disk in the same directory as the above processes seen on the host - this list is not exhaustive. Huntress has not manually downloaded the files to confirm they contain credentials; however, due to the naming, password content is likely and your team should consider investigating.
If a threat actor gains access to this host and discovers a password file (whether locally or on a shared drive), these credentials can be used to launch follow-on attacks against the organization or to laterally move across the network. Additionally, password storage in this manner may negatively impact audits for mandatory security standards in certain industries.

Lead Signal Information:
------------------------
Signal Name: Potential Unsecured Credentials In Files
Detected At: 2025-11-17 01:35:13 UTC
Start Time: 2025-11-17 01:27:47 UTC
Command: "C:\Program Files\WindowsApps\Microsoft.WindowsNotepad_11.2508.38.0_x64__8wekyb3d8bbwe\Notepad\Notepad.exe" "C:\Users\donaldh\OneDrive - JDH Pacific\Desktop\VPN PASSWORD.txt"
Executable: C:\Program Files\WindowsApps\Microsoft.WindowsNotepad_11.2508.38.0_x64__8wekyb3d8bbwe\Notepad\Notepad.exe
Process ID: 88941aaa-c354-11f0-86ea-f44efce4c1d3
Parent Process: C:\Windows\explorer.exe
User: donaldh

All investigated signals can be found in the Huntress Platform: https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/infection_reports/1802192#signals-investigated-tab

-------------------------
Thanks again for trusting Huntress and please don't hesitate to reach out to incidents@huntress.io if you have any questions.
all_recent.subject: LOW - Incident on Donald-WS-Home (JDH - JDH Pacific)
all_recent.summary: LOW - Potentially Insecure Credentials: Credentials In Files on Donald-WS-Home (JDH - JDH Pacific)

all_recent.body:
*** The Huntress SOC disabled Active Remediations for this incident. Please read below to understand why. ***


Host: JDH-HQ-PC-10 - https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/agents/11359374
Organization: JDH Pacific
Tags: None
Security Products: Trend Micro Security Agent, Malwarebytes, CrowdStrike (CSFalconService)

Incident Report: https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/infection_reports/1406481
Severity: High

Investigative Summary
---------------------
[WARNING]
Please review this incident report to understand what was identified before remediating. There may be unknown malicious processes, files, or other changes made to the host that remain undetected. Restoring from a known good backup or clean OS install is the only way to ensure a complete host-level remediation. While the Assisted Remediation service is an option, it will only remove specific items documented in this report.

Huntress detected the following on this host:
- Dridex : This malware is known to distribute ransomware, banking trojans, and other types of malware.


The Huntress SOC identified the Malware Family as Dridex.


Remediation Instructions
------------------------
* Dridex uses legitimate Windows files which normally are located in c:\windows\system32 and creates a copy of the file in new directory. A malicious DLL file is then placed within the new directory. When the executable is launched it loads the malicious DLL.

To remediate, run the following commands from an administrative command prompt (cmd.exe) and perform the actions below:
- schtasks.exe /End /TN "Ennqzgxhbzpfpa"
- schtasks.exe /Delete /TN "Ennqzgxhbzpfpa" /F
- schtasks.exe /End /TN "Dkmehsdndstha"
- schtasks.exe /Delete /TN "Dkmehsdndstha" /F

- Remove the "c:\windows\system32\xexrk\gamepanel.exe" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Audit the "c:\windows\system32\xexrk" directory for other suspicious files; remove the directory if not required.
- Remove the "c:\windows\system32\rc98\wextract.exe" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Audit the "c:\windows\system32\rc98" directory for other suspicious files; remove the directory if not required.
- Remove the "c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\xbyn\cloudstoragewizard.exe" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Audit the "c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\xbyn" directory for other suspicious files; remove the directory if not required.
- Remove the "c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\start menu\programs\startup\mgqmxvoje.lnk" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Remove the "c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\start menu\programs\startup\amzvfk.lnk" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Remove the "c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\hjzsc\mblctr.exe" file and ensure it is not recreated.
- Audit the "c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\hjzsc" directory for other suspicious files; remove the directory if not required.

* You can also use the Task Scheduler GUI to remove the tasks if you prefer:
Start Menu > Control Panel > System and Security > Administrative Tools > Task Scheduler
Remove the following tasks:
- "Ennqzgxhbzpfpa"
- "Dkmehsdndstha"

Footholds
---------
Foothold 1 - https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/autoruns/35380428
Name: Dkmehsdndstha
Task File: C:\WINDOWS\System32\Tasks\Dkmehsdndstha
Command: C:\WINDOWS\system32\xEXrK\GamePanel.exe
Username: S-1-5-21-2000478354-1214440339-682003330-2167
File Path: c:\windows\system32\xexrk\gamepanel.exe

Foothold 2 - https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/autoruns/35380429
Name: Ennqzgxhbzpfpa
Task File: C:\WINDOWS\System32\Tasks\Ennqzgxhbzpfpa
Command: C:\WINDOWS\system32\RC98\wextract.exe
Username: S-1-5-21-2000478354-1214440339-682003330-2167
File Path: c:\windows\system32\rc98\wextract.exe

Foothold 3 - https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/autoruns/35380426
Startup Path: c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\start menu\programs\startup\amzvfk.lnk
Command: C:\Users\account.JDH\AppData\Roaming\xBYn\CloudStorageWizard.exe 
File Path: c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\xbyn\cloudstoragewizard.exe

Foothold 4 - https://technijian.huntress.io/org/429259/autoruns/35380427
Startup Path: c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\start menu\programs\startup\mgqmxvoje.lnk
Command: C:\Users\account.JDH\AppData\Roaming\hjzSC\mblctr.exe 
File Path: c:\users\account.jdh\appdata\roaming\hjzsc\mblctr.exe



-------------------------
Thanks again for trusting Huntress and please don't hesitate to reach out to incidents@huntress.io if you have any questions.
